content.problemStatement:
Chef loves Chess and has thus invented a new piece named "Disabled King".

Let's denote the cell at the intersection of the iii-th column from the left and jjj-th row from the top by (i,j)(i, j)(i,j).

If he is currently in cell (x,y)(x,y)(x,y), the disabled king can move to the following positions in one move (provided that he remains in the chessboard):


(x,y+1)(x,y+1)(x,y+1)
(x,y−1)(x,y-1)(x,y−1)
(x+1,y+1)(x+1,y+1)(x+1,y+1)
(x+1,y−1)(x+1,y-1)(x+1,y−1)
(x−1,y+1)(x-1,y+1)(x−1,y+1)
(x−1,y−1)(x-1,y-1)(x−1,y−1)
In short, the Disabled King cannot move horizontally.

In an N×NN \times NN×N chessboard, the Disabled King is currently situated at the top-left corner (cell (1,1)(1, 1)(1,1)) and wants to reach the top-right corner (cell (N,1)(N, 1)(N,1)). Determine the minimum number of moves in which the task can be accomplished.


Input Format

The first line will contain TTT, the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
Each test case contains a single integer NNN in a separate line.


Output Format
Output the minimum number of moves to get from the top-left cell to the top-right one.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a single integer N denoting the size of the board.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line with the minimum number of moves required for the Disabled King to travel from (1,1) to (N,1).

sampleTestCases.explanation: For N=2 (even) answer = 2. For N=3 (odd) answer = 3-1 = 2.
N=4 (even) → 4 moves. N=5 (odd) → 5-1=4 moves. N=6 (even) → 6 moves.

solution.approach:
The king can only change its column by moving diagonally, which also changes the row by ±1. To end on row 1 we must perform an even number of diagonal moves, alternating between up‑right and down‑right. Two such moves shift the column by 2 and bring the king back to row 1. Hence we can cover the distance in pairs of moves. If N is odd we need (N‑1) moves (the last column is reached after the final up‑right move). If N is even we need N moves because after reaching column N‑1 we are on row 1, then one extra vertical move is required to stay on row 1 while moving to column N, which adds one more move, making the total equal to N. The resulting formula is:
    answer = N            if N is even
    answer = N - 1        if N is odd
